summary: Charity Majors argues that companies should build fair, transparent compensation systems and invest in ongoing career development rather than relying on heroic counter-offers when employees threaten to leave. She contends that healthy turnover is natural, that managers should proactively identify superstars and single points of failure, and that exceptions to compensation frameworks—while occasionally necessary—compound into cultural damage and cynicism.
key_points: Stay at your job as long as it fulfills your career priorities, but don't stay out of guilt or fear
Managers should proactively ensure roles remain great opportunities and be honest when they're not
Some employee turnover is natural and healthy—zero attrition can signal cult-like dysfunction
Companies should distinguish between superstars (retain out of joy) and SPOFs (retain out of fear), and have plans for both
Fair compensation requires structural work: pay bands, calibrations, equity audits, and refusing to reward brinksmanship
Counter-offers and exceptions to comp frameworks impose compounding costs to credibility and culture
money_quotes: The harder your employer is affirmatively working to do right by you, the fewer heroics they will be willing or able to do to retain you.
If the company's future depends irrevocably on the continued employment of any individual person, the company's leaders are fucking up, full stop.
If superstars are the people you want to keep out of joy, SPOFs can be the people you need to keep out of fear.
Any time you make an exception to keep someone, you risk breaking your commitments to everyone else.
People work at jobs for money, but not only money.
key_insight: Sustainable employee retention comes from building fair, transparent systems for compensation and career development—not from heroic counter-offers that reward brinksmanship and erode organizational trust.
